Project Overview

Booking flights
should feel effortless.

FlightKite is a UK-based flight booking platform. Their original booking flow had accumulated too many steps over time — users were dropping off before completing bookings, especially on mobile.

I was brought in to audit the existing UX, identify friction points, and redesign the booking journey end-to-end — from search to seat selection to payment confirmation.

The result was a restructured flow that cut unnecessary steps, simplified decision points, and made the experience feel fast and intuitive on any device.

UI DesignUX ResearchWireframing Figma PrototypingMobile-FirstDeveloper Handoff

Full Design

The complete experience

↑ Hover to scroll through the full design

Challenge & Solution

What was broken.
What we fixed.

🔴 The Problem

Too many steps, too much friction

The original booking flow required users to navigate through 9+ steps before completing a booking. The mobile experience was particularly painful — form fields were hard to tap, loading states were unclear, and users frequently had to backtrack. Drop-off rates were high at the payment stage.

🟢 The Solution

Streamlined, mobile-first redesign

I restructured the user journey by consolidating related steps, introducing smart defaults and auto-fill patterns, and redesigning the information architecture so users could see their progress clearly. The mobile layout was rebuilt with thumb-friendly touch targets and an inline seat selector that eliminated a full sub-flow.

🔍 Research

User flows & heuristic audit

I mapped the existing user journey, identified the 4 biggest drop-off points, ran a heuristic evaluation against Nielsen's 10 principles, and benchmarked against 3 competitor booking flows (Skyscanner, Google Flights, EasyJet) to identify best-in-class patterns to adapt.

✅ Outcome

A cleaner, faster booking journey

The redesigned flow reduced the booking steps from 9 to 5, introduced a real-time progress indicator, and improved mobile tap targets across all interactive elements. The design system components built for this project were reused across other FlightKite product pages.

Design Process

How I approached it

01

Discover

Audited the existing flow, mapped drop-off points, interviewed stakeholders, and benchmarked competitor booking experiences.

02

Define

Created a revised user journey map highlighting the 4 critical friction points and defined success metrics with the client.

03

Design

Wireframed 3 flow variations, tested with the client, then built high-fidelity Figma screens with an interactive prototype.

04

Deliver

Handed off annotated specs, built a component library, and worked with the dev team through the first implementation sprint.

Results & Impact

The numbers that matter

9→5

Booking steps reduced from 9 to 5, cutting completion time significantly

↑40%

Improved mobile task completion rate through touch-optimised redesign

1 DS

Shared design system built — reused across all FlightKite product pages

Key Learnings

What this project taught me

01

Mobile-first isn't optional for booking flows. Users completing high-stakes tasks like booking flights are often on mobile with limited time. Designing for the smallest screen first forced better decisions that improved the desktop experience too.

02

Progress visibility kills drop-off. Adding a clear, persistent progress indicator was one of the highest-impact changes — users who can see how far they've come are much more likely to complete a multi-step flow.

03

Design systems save client money. By building reusable components from the start, the client was able to apply the new design language across 3 other pages without additional design work.
